coredata.dc:description: The massive use and abuse of antibiotics has given birth to drug-resistant bacteria and emergence of severe bacterial infections in people. Intriguingly, light activated antimicrobial activity i.e. antimicrobial photodynamic therapy has gained enhanced therapeutic potential and efficacy in killing the bacterial cells. Therefore, the present study investigates the efficacy of employing advanced nanocomposites for biocidal inhibition of bacterial growth through enhanced sterilization capabilities. Specifically, dopamine functionalized CuS NPs combined with carbon dots (CDs@Dop-CuS) were selected for their remarkable photodynamic potential when used in conjunction with methylene blue (MB). Comparative and synergistic application of Dop-CuS, CDs, and MB revealed the superior singlet oxygen generation potency of MB/CDs@Dop-CuS. Furthermore, the structural changes in the E. coli bacterial cells membrane were elucidated through scanning electron microscopy imaging, demonstrating superior inhibition rates of MB/CDs@Dop-CuS compared to MB alone under 660 nm irradiation. The commendable docking score and binding potential of CDs@Dop-CuS nanocomposites obtained via molecular docking simulations further validate their efficacy as photosensitizers against E. coli bacterial cells. Thus, this novel MB/CDs@Dop-CuS photosensitizer provides enhanced efficacy as an alternative to traditional antibiotic treatments, offering a superior disinfection for public utility services and addressing the growing issue of drug-resistant bacterial infections.

head.ce:title: Photosensitizer conjugated carbon dots@dopamine-copper sulfide nanocomposites: Advanced photoactive agents for antibacterial applications through experimental and theoretical insights

ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Department of Chemistry and Centre of Advanced Studies in Chemistry, Panjab University, Chandigarh 160014, India

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: Department of Chemistry and Centre of Advanced Studies in Chemistry, Panjab University

ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: Department of Chemistry and Centre of Advanced Studies in Chemistry, Panjab University, Chandigarh 160014, India

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: The massive use and abuse of antibiotics has given birth to drug-resistant bacteria and emergence of severe bacterial infections in people. Intriguingly, light activated antimicrobial activity i.e. antimicrobial photodynamic therapy has gained enhanced therapeutic potential and efficacy in killing the bacterial cells. Therefore, the present study investigates the efficacy of employing advanced nanocomposites for biocidal inhibition of bacterial growth through enhanced sterilization capabilities. Specifically, dopamine functionalized CuS NPs combined with carbon dots (CDs@Dop-CuS) were selected for their remarkable photodynamic potential when used in conjunction with methylene blue (MB). Comparative and synergistic application of Dop-CuS, CDs, and MB revealed the superior singlet oxygen generation potency of MB/CDs@Dop-CuS. Furthermore, the structural changes in the E. coli bacterial cells membrane were elucidated through scanning electron microscopy imaging, demonstrating superior inhibition rates of MB/CDs@Dop-CuS compared to MB alone under 660 nm irradiation. The commendable docking score and binding potential of CDs@Dop-CuS nanocomposites obtained via molecular docking simulations further validate their efficacy as photosensitizers against E. coli bacterial cells. Thus, this novel MB/CDs@Dop-CuS photosensitizer provides enhanced efficacy as an alternative to traditional antibiotic treatments, offering a superior disinfection for public utility services and addressing the growing issue of drug-resistant bacterial infections.

ce:data-availability.ce:para: Data will be made available on request.

ce:section.ce:para: The rise of multidrug-resistant bacteria has posed a substantial challenge to the efficacy of commercially available antibiotics for clinical purposes [1,2]. According to data from the Centers for Disease Control and Prevention (CDC) in the United States, approximately 2.8 million antibiotic-resistant infections occur annually in the U.S., resulting in around 35,000 deaths due to infections caused by multidrug-resistant bacteria [3]. Therefore, the discovery of innovative, economical, and non-lethal methods to combat the deadly threat posed by multidrug-resistant bacteria is crucial for maintaining human health [4–6]. In this context, photodynamic therapy (PDT) is gaining increasing attention over other antimicrobial treatments due to its simplicity, minimal invasiveness, lower toxicity, and reduced likelihood of developing drug resistance [7–9]. The fundamental principle of PDT involves the production of singlet oxygen species when a photosensitizer (PS) is irradiated with laser light of a specific wavelength [10]. To date, various materials such as porphyrins, xanthenes, monoterpenes, chlorins, furocoumarins, and dyes have been extensively used as active photosensitizers for generating reactive oxygen species (ROS) [11,12]. The use of dyes in PDT has received considerable attention due to their inherent activity and easy elimination from the body [13–15]. Among different types of dyes, Methylene Blue (MB) is a well-recognized photosensitizer with a high propensity to produce a significant amount of singlet oxygen under visible light illumination. However, the application of MB in PDT requires high concentrations and prolonged laser exposure, which can cause tissue damage and limit its clinical use [16]. Therefore, the development of stable, advanced, and easy-to-use materials with tunable PDT potential and low toxicity to biological environments is highly desirable. Specifically, synthetic polymers, liposomes, polymeric micelles, and nanomaterials are among the most promising carriers in PDT [17,18]. The stimuli-responsive nature and low toxicity of nanomaterials make them effective carriers for encapsulating commercially available photosensitizers [19–21]. Consequently, designing highly potent nanocarriers for targeted action, combined with improved therapeutic potential of PSs, is a highly favored approach to address the challenge of multidrug-resistant bacteria [22]. Among the diverse range of nanophototherapeutic agents, carbon dots (CDs) have received significant attention due to their exceptional biocompatibility, photoactive characteristics, and robust surface functionalization capabilities [23,24]. Notably, CDs have also emerged as prominent metal-free catalysts, characterized by their straightforward fabrication methods and elevated thermal stability. Currently, CDs are being recognized as innovative nanocarriers endowed with compelling physicochemical attributes, including advanced optical, photoluminescent, and catalytic functionalities [25,26]. For example, Chen et al. reported on six different types of carbon dots derived from the carbonization/hydrothermal processing of various plant species, exploring their roles in cell imaging and cytotoxicity [27]. Surface modifications of carbon dots can impart additional functionalities, thereby enhancing their therapeutic efficacy. However, there is limited literature on the use of CDs for photodynamic antibacterial activities. This limitation is mainly due to the low production of reactive oxygen species (ROS) in CDs when exposed to near-infrared (NIR) light, which is insufficient for complete bacterial inhibition [28]. To overcome this challenge, combining CDs with p-type semiconducting nanomaterials that have high NIR absorbing abilities can significantly enhance ROS production using low-energy lasers, leading to complete bacterial inhibition. Among the diverse range of p-type semiconducting materials, copper sulfide nanoparticles (CuS NPs) have shown a high potency for light absorption, which can further influence ROS production. The remarkable structural integrity, coupled with the outstanding optical and photocatalytic attributes of CuS NPs, has driven their applications in solar cells, photothermal activities, and biomedical and therapeutic applications [29–31]. However, the high agglomeration rate and surface defects in bare CuS have limited their widespread application in wastewater treatment [32]. Consequently, surface modification of CuS with templating agents is considered an effective strategy in environmental remediation applications [33]. Furthermore, the surface functionalization of CuS with dopamine (Dop) has been found to improve the long-term stability and dispersion ability of bare CuS NPs. Therefore, we propose a novel heterojunction between CDs and Dop-functionalized CuS for photodynamic antimicrobial activities.

In this study, we have rationally designed a more targeted, cost effective and efficient nanocomposite by integrating dopamine-functionalized copper sulfide (CuS) with carbon dots (CDs), resulting in the formation of robust CDs@Dop-CuS nanocomposite. The proposed structures were theoretically investigated using density functional theory (DFT) calculations, which revealed that the synthesised CDs@Dop-CuS nanocomposite possesses the lowest energy gap (0.0635 eV), indicating maximum stability. Additionally, the nanocomposite exhibited significantly enhanced antibacterial activities compared to their individual components, as corroborated by molecular docking calculations. The synergistic effect and enhanced efficiency of the nanocomposite facilitates a more potent antibacterial effect at lower doses, in contrast to metal-based nanomaterials, which are associated with long-term environmental impacts and higher disposal costs. This methodology offers a simple and cost-effective approach to provide an effective carrier for commercially available photosensitizers, such as Methylene Blue (MB) to improve their stability and potency. The outcomes highlight the potential of the developed nanocomposite as novel nano photosensitizers, significantly improving their photo-inactivation effects on bacterial growth. Moreover, the low production cost, coupled with excellent stability and dispersibility in aqueous media, provides a superior alternative for disinfection in public utility services.

Copper chloride salt (CuCl2.2H2O), Dopamine (Dop), Methylene Blue (MB), Sodium thiosulfate (Na2S2O3) and Uric acid (UA) were purchased from Sigma Aldrich with purity > 98 %. Absolute Ethanol (99.9 % purity) was purchased from Changshu Yangyuan Chemicals, China. Nutrient broth and nutrient agar were procured from Himedia with 99 % purity for antimicrobial activities. Aegle marmelos (Bael Patra) fruit was obtained from Botanical Garden, Panjab University, Chandigarh. A Gram-negative bacterial striating of E. coli was obtained from Department of Environmental studies, Panjab University, Chandigarh. Milli-Q water was used for each experiment. All the chemicals were utilized as received without any further refinement.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Fabrication of dopamine functionalized CuS nanoparticles
ce:section.ce:para: A highly economical and rapid microwave-assisted route was chosen to synthesize dopamine (Dop) functionalized CuS nanoparticles (CuS NPs) [34]. Briefly, a 100 mL solution of 0.02 M CuCl2·2 H2O and a 0.015 M solution of Na2S2O3 were prepared in aqueous media. Both solutions were sonicated for 5 minutes, followed by magnetic stirring at 350 rpm for 20 minutes. After complete homogenization, the two suspensions were mixed together with continuous stirring at 350 rpm. Subsequently, a 100 mL solution of 0.01 M Dop was added to the mixture. The resultant solution was then subjected to microwave irradiation (IFB 800 W) at 90°C for 5 minutes. The color change from light yellow to brownish black indicated the formation of Dop-CuS NPs ( Fig. 1a). The suspension was then centrifuged for 15 minutes at 6000 rpm, and the supernatant was removed. The brownish black nanosuspension was then dried in an oven at 50°C.

A greener approach using the fruit extract of Aegle marmelos was applied for the synthesis of carbon dots (CDs) [35,36]. Initially, the fruit extract of Aegle marmelos was thoroughly washed with deionized water and ethanol, followed by the separation of its fruit exocarp by peeling with a knife. The finely chopped and separated exocarp was then washed again with water and dried for 2 hours at 100°C. Subsequently, 10 g of dried fruit extract was placed in a silica crucible and subjected to thermal calcination in an oven at 250°C for 90 minutes. The resulting powder was finely ground using a mortar and pestle, and 1 g of the resulting powder was added to 500 mL of water. The solution was stirred for 24 hours at room temperature and then centrifuged at 12,000 rpm to settle the sediments (Fig. 1a). The supernatant was passed through a 0.22 μm filter to remove larger particles from the solution. The obtained liquid was further purified using dialysis bag tubing with a molecular weight cut-off (MWCO) of 10,000 Da. The purified carbon dots (CDs) were then checked under a 365 nm UV illuminator to ensure the photoluminescence activities of the formed CDs.

ce:section.ce:section-title: One pot synthesis of CDs@Dop-CuS NPs
ce:section.ce:para: The desired nanocomposite of CDs@Dop-CuS was synthesized using a one-step solvothermal procedure. Initially, 100 mg of prepared CDs and 50 mg of Dop-CuS NPs were separately dispersed in 50 mL of water. Both solutions were stirred for 1 hour at 400 rpm. Next, the solution of CDs was added to the Dop-CuS NPs suspension under vigorous stirring conditions at room temperature (Fig. 1a). The final solution was stirred for about 12 hours, followed by centrifugation and drying at 50°C.

The size and morphological features of the nanocomposite were acquired with Hitachi H-7500 high resolution transmission electron microscope (HR-TEM) at 100 kV and JEOL JSM 7500 F field emission scanning electron microscope (FE-SEM) along with elemental composition analysis at 20 keV. The UV–vis. absorption spectra were obtained using LABINDIA UV-Visible (UV–vis.) spectrophotometer in the range of 200–800 nm via quartz cuvette of 1 cm path length. The surface charge distribution was obtained using Delsa™ Nano C, Beckman Coulter at 25 °C. The thermal calcination treatment was conducted on AICIL muffle furnace. The heating and stirring process was carried on IKA C-MAG HS7 stirrer. The structural modification of nanocomposite was acquired using Perkin Elmer (RXI) Fourier transform infrared (FTIR) spectrometer instrument over the range of 400 cm−1 to 4000 cm−1. The thermal stability was studied by obtaining TGA (Thermogravimetric analysis) spectrum via SDT Q-600 instrument and DSC (Differential scanning calorimeter) spectrum was acquired through METTLER TOLEDO (Q20) instrument. The XRD (X-ray diffraction) measurements were computed with Panalytical X’Pert Pro XRD diffractometer with a scan rate of 2 deg. min−1 and Cukα radiation in the 2θ range from 5 to 90°. The crystallite size was analysed from the width of diffractogram peaks using Debye Scherer formula:
(1) D = 0.89λ /β Cosθ
Here, D symbolizes the mean crystallite size, λ denotes the incident X-ray wavelength, β represents the full width at half maximum (FWHM) of the diffraction peak, which is expressed in radians, and θ stands for the position of the peak in the diffractogram.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Antimicrobial activity via colony formation method
ce:section.ce:para: The antimicrobial activities of Dop-CuS NPs, CDs and CDs@Dop-CuS nanocomposite were performed via colony formation method (CFU) to evaluate their antimicrobial potential against E. coli bacterial cells. Briefly a Gram-negative isolate of bacteria i.e. E. coli was inoculated in nutrient broth and placed in an incubator, maintained at 37°C for 12 h [37,38]. All the samples and glasswares were autoclaved prior to use. Solid media prepared on Petri dishes using nutrient agar served as a growth medium for estimating the antimicrobial activity of nanoparticles. A volume of 50 μL of the revived bacterial strain was diluted (10−4) and spread uniformly on the agar plates, with one broth solution serving as control while the others were perfused with different concentrations of chosen nanoparticles. The bacterial cells were cultured on agar petri dishes under optimal growth conditions, and the efficacy of each nanoparticle in terms of bacteriostatic abilities was assessed by quantifying the number of colonies formed following a 24 h incubation period. During the analysis, environmental factors, including temperature and humidity, were carefully regulated to ensure optimal bacterial growth and to enhance the reproducibility of the results. Each experiment was done in triplicates and average results were plotted.

To prepare the Methylene Blue (MB) loaded CDs@Dop-CuS nanocomposite, 1 mL of a 30 μM MB solution was added to 0.5 mL of the CDs@Dop-CuS nanocomposite solution (1 mg/mL) and stirred at 450 rpm for 12 h [39]. The resultant solution was then subjected to centrifugation, dialysis, and drying to obtain the MB/CDs@Dop-CuS nanocomposite. The loading efficiency was estimated by measuring the fluorescence intensity of MB before and after loading with the CDs@Dop-CuS nanocomposite. The encapsulation efficiency was calculated using the following equation:
(2) Encapsulation efficacy (%) = (Imax-Io)/Imax×100
Here Imax and Io denote the fluorescence intensity of MB without and with loading onto CDs@Dop-CuS nanocomposite.

ce:section.ce:section-title: In vitro antibacterial photodynamic therapy via CFU
ce:section.ce:para:
The antibacterial photodynamic therapy was performed via colony formation method (CFU) by taking E. coli as Gram negative bacterial strain [40]. For the analysis, E. coli was inoculated and grown in nutrient broth overnight at 37°C. The bacteria were diluted to 10−4 dilution and were incubated with bare photosensitizer (Methylene blue dye) as well as MB loaded CDs@Dop-CuS nanocomposite. Subsequently, the effect of dark and laser (660 nm) were studied via irradiation for 30 min. Aliquots of the sample were withdrawn, and 50 μL solution of each bacterial suspension was spread onto agar plates. The bacterial survival rate was recorded after 24 h of incubation at 37°C using CFU counting method. The viability count for each treatment (control, dark and light) was done in triplicate plates with standard deviation represented by error bars in each graph. The relative viability of bacterial cell was calculated using following equation:
(3)
Relative cell viability =
N
t
N
c
× 100
Where Nc and Nt represents the number of colonies obtained from control and treated bacterial cells. Further, the antibacterial efficacy was approximated by bacterial inhibition percentage (BIP), as follows:
(4)
BIP =
C control − C experiment
C control
× 100
Where Ccontrol and Cexperimental refer to the number colonies appeared in control and experimental batch respectively.

The morphological alterations in E. coli cells were evaluated by comparing cellular morphology between cultures inoculated with and without bare methylene blue, as well as those treated with MB-loaded CDs@Dop-CuS nanocomposite. This analysis was conducted using SEM [41]. To prepare the bacterial samples, 15 mL of revived bacterial culture was incubated separately with and without the MB-loaded CDs@Dop-CuS nanocomposite for 3 hours at 37°C. An additional 15 mL culture was inoculated with a solution of MB dye. Following this, the samples were irradiated with laser light for 30 minutes. After irradiation, the samples were harvested by centrifugation at 3500 rpm for 10 minutes and washed three times with phosphate-buffered saline (PBS). The collected cells were resuspended in a 2.5 % glutaraldehyde solution and frozen at −4°C for 1 hour. The fixed bacterial cells were then dehydrated using a series of ethanol solutions, sequentially ranging from 50 % to 100 %, with each step performed through centrifugation at 4000 rpm. The dehydrated cell pellets were placed on copper tape and coated with platinum at 25°C for further imaging.

The geometric optimizations of the synthesized nanomaterials i.e. CDs, @Dop-CuS nanoparticles, CDs@Dop-CuS nanocomposite were performed using Density Functional Theory (DFT) on Gaussian 09 package by means of Coulomb-attenuating three-parameter Becke, Lee-Yang-Parr (CAM-B3LYP). Particularly, 6–311+G* and LAND2DZ basis sets have been employed to study the geometrical structure of CDs and @Dop-CuS NPs respectively [42]. Additionally, the orientation manifolds between CDs and Dop-CuS nanoparticles were investigated by applying the LAND2DZ basis set. Based on the computed HOMO (Highest Occupied Molecular Orbital), LUMO (Lowest Unoccupied Molecular Orbital) values, the band gap of the molecule was calculated using following equation:
(5) ∆E = ELUMO- EHOMO

The structural optimization of the nanomaterials was employed without causing any symmetry constraint to the molecules. The molecular orbitals, electron density maps, and mulliken charge distribution of the structures were visualized on Gauss view program.

To predict the optimal conformation and determine the binding affinity between the receptor and ligands, molecular docking was employed. The studies involve the binding efficiency of CDs@Dop-Cus nanocomposite with the enzymes i.e. β-lactamase (4KZ6) and DNA gyrase (7P2X), found in E. coli bacterial cells. Initially, water molecules and heteroatoms were removed from the protein molecules followed by addition of Kollman charges. For ligand molecule, Gasteiger charges were computed and the torsion tree root was chosen and detected. The output files for both the receptor and ligand were generated in PDBQT format and Lamarckian Genetic Algorithm (LGA) capability was utilized for docking studies. The docked structure with minimum binding energy was determined using docking genetic algorithm search. The binding configuration and visualization of structures was done on BIOVIA Discovery Studio.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Optical and structural characterization of CDs@Dop-CuS nanocomposite
ce:section.ce:para: The nanocomposite synthesized via a one-step solvothermal procedure was analyzed both theoretically and structurally using DFT calculations and various characterization techniques (Fig. 1).

To enhance understanding of electron delocalization and optimize the molecular structures of CDs, Dop-CuS nanoparticles, and CDs@Dop-CuS nanocomposite, quantum mechanical calculations were conducted using the Gaussian 09 software suite (Fig. 1b, c, d). Fig. 1d illustrates the energy gap of the CDs@Dop-CuS nanocomposite, showing that the HOMO resides on the aromatic ring of the CDs, while the LUMO is localized on the surface of the Dop-CuS nanoparticles [43]. The energy-minimized structure of the nanocomposite indicates interaction between the Dop-CuS nanoparticles and the aromatic ring of the CDs via the amide groups present on the CDs. Additionally, the binding behavior of both structures and the energy gap between the HOMO and LUMO were calculated. The results reveal a significantly reduced band gap value of 0.0635 eV for CDs@Dop-CuS, compared to 0.0795 eV for bare CDs and 0.0685 eV for Dop-CuS nanoparticles. This notable decrease in the band gap of the nanocomposite suggests enhanced stability of the CDs in the complex state. Using the Gaussian 09 suite of programs, electrostatic potential maps for all structures were generated, with results depicted in Fig. 1e. As illustrated in Fig. 1eiii, the electrophilic potential on the Cu atoms demonstrates a strong attraction to the high electronic charge density of the nucleophilic oxygen in the amide group. The Mulliken charge distribution, shown in Fig. S1, reveals complex behavior due to the presence of nitrogen- and oxygen-containing functional moieties within the CDs ring. The synthesized nanomaterials were further characterized using various techniques, including XRD, FTIR, UV-Vis. spectroscopy, FESEM, and HRTEM. XRD analysis is a crucial structural technique for confirming the formation of nanoparticles. Major diffraction peaks were observed at 25.54°, 27.30°, 29.68°, 31.75°, 42.11°, 45.43°, 49.26°, and 54.10°, corresponding to the (100), (101), (102), (103), (105), (008), (110), and (114) facets of Cu and sulfur (S) in the Dop-CuS NPs. The absence of additional peaks indicates the high purity of the Dop-CuS NPs. Using the Debye-Scherrer equation, the average crystallite size of the Dop-CuS NPs was calculated to be approximately 19.15 nm. The XRD spectrum of the CDs confirmed their amorphous nature, with a major peak centered at 2θ value of 21.42°, corresponding to the 002 facets. The single broad peak observed in the CDs synthesized from the fruit extract of Aegle marmelos supports their poor crystallinity and is attributed to the presence of more oxygen-containing moieties in the formed particles [44]. For the CDs@Dop-CuS nanocomposite, XRD diffraction peaks were identified at 23.03°, 25.48°, 27.36°, 29.21°, 31.34°, 42.40°, 45.76°, 49.86°, and 53.97°, corresponding to the (002), (100), (101), (102), (103), (105), (008), (110), and (114) lattice planes. Notably, the peak at 21.42° for the CDs shifted to 23.02°, indicating alterations in the graphitic structure of the CDs and changes in interlayer spacing. Furthermore, the zeta potential measurements revealed values of −33.45 mV for Dop-CuS NPs, 19.08 mV for CDs, and −12.22 mV for the CDs@Dop-CuS nanocomposite, further supporting the stability and interaction characteristics of the synthesized materials.

The absorption spectrum of Dop-CuS NPs exhibited three major absorption bands at 213.3 nm, 318.09 nm, and 588.38 nm ( Fig. 2bi). In contrast, the synthesized CDs displayed two distinct peaks at 254.7 nm and 320.16 nm, corresponding to the π-π* transition of the CC bond and the n-π* transition of the CO bond, respectively. The UV–vis. absorption peaks for both systems facilitated significant overlapping, resulting in the final nanocomposite exhibiting peaks at 218.32 nm and 311.96 nm (Fig. 2biii). The FTIR spectrum of Dop-CuS NPs revealed a sharp peak at 3438.6 cm⁻¹, indicative of -OH stretching (Fig. 2cii). Similarly, the CDs and the CDs@Dop-CuS nanocomposite showed -OH stretching vibrations at 3432.6 cm⁻¹ and 3436.2 cm⁻¹, along with shoulders at 2925.3 cm⁻¹ and 2922.7 cm⁻¹, which indicate the presence of N-H stretching vibrations (Fig. 2ci & ciii) [45]. Additional peaks in the FTIR spectrum of Dop-CuS NPs at 1620.9, 1150.5, and 600.2 cm⁻¹ were attributed to CC, C-O-C, and Cu-S stretching vibrations, respectively (Fig. 2ci). For the CDs, peaks at 1625.4, 1434.5, 1107.9, 876.8, and 615.4 cm⁻¹ corresponded to CC, C-N, C-O-C stretching, and CC, C-H bending vibrations (Fig. 2cii). The FTIR spectrum of the CDs@Dop-CuS nanocomposite, shown in Fig. 2ciii, featured strong absorption peaks at 1623.5, 1392, and 1150.6 cm⁻¹, consistent with CC, C-N, and C-O-C stretching vibrations. Additionally, peaks at 656.2 and 601.4 cm⁻¹ were attributed to C-H bending and Cu-O stretching vibrations, respectively. These findings are in agreement with the results obtained from other structural characterization techniques, such as FTIR and XRD, further confirming the successful synthesis and structural integrity of the Dop-CuS NPs and their composites.

The thermal stability of the synthesized materials was evaluated using TGA and DSC analysis. The TGA spectrum for Dop-CuS NPs demonstrated a 27.94 % weight loss through a two-step degradation process, exhibiting a lower onset degradation rate compared to the CDs (Fig. S2ai) [46]. The weight loss occurred in two distinct temperature ranges: 44°-539°C, resulting in a weight loss of 12.01 % (0.9937 mg), and 539°-942°C, accounting for 15.93 % (1.317 mg). The first weight loss peak is attributed to moisture removal and sulfur content, while the second peak indicates the oxidation of Cu to CuO. For the CDs, the first weight loss of 12.49 % (0.4954 mg) was observed in the temperature range of 26–105°C, primarily due to the loss of water molecules and surface impurities (Fig. S2aii). The second weight loss occurred between 275 and 516°C, amounting to 32.46 % (1.286 mg), reflecting the degradation of surface functional groups linked to the graphitic structure of the CDs [47]. Additionally, a significant weight loss of 42.76 % (1.695 mg) was noted in the temperature range of 745–948°C, attributed to the loss of organic moieties and disruption of the graphitic carbon structure (Fig. S2aii). The DSC curves for both Dop-CuS NPs and CDs revealed endothermic heat release profiles, confirming the thermal behavior of the materials during degradation (Fig. S2bi and S2bii).

The photostability of the nanocarriers is a crucial factor for their application in photodynamic processes. Therefore, the photostability of Dop-CuS NPs, CDs, and the CDs@Dop-CuS nanocomposite was evaluated under various light and dark conditions as control measurements ( Fig. 3ai-aiii). The results indicated that all nanomaterials exhibited consistent signals in the UV-Vis spectra when irradiated with different light sources. Notably, no spectral changes were observed for any of the synthesized particles upon UV light irradiation (Fig. 3bi-biii). Additionally, the systems were exposed to visible light at a specific wavelength of 660 nm to assess the degradation potential of the nanomaterials (Fig. 3ci-ciii). Analysis of the data revealed that none of the tested conditions resulted in significant spectral changes across the different nanomaterials. These findings suggest that the prepared nanomaterials possess high stability and are non-photosensitive when subjected to light sources of varying wavelengths.

The morphological characteristics of Dop-CuS NPs and CDs were further investigated using SEM and TEM. The HRTEM image of Dop-CuS NPs revealed the overlapping of two longitudinal CuS NPs molecules coated with a single layer of dopamine ( Fig. 4ai). The length of these particles ranged from 15 to 20 nm, while their width was between 4 and 5 nm [48]. The selected area electron diffraction (SAED) pattern for Dop-CuS NPs showed bright spots, confirming the formation of highly crystalline particles (Fig. S3a). The FESEM images for Dop-CuS NPs corroborated the longitudinal, rod-like morphology of CuS NPs. Due to the adhesive nature of the dopamine coating on the CuS NPs, significant agglomeration was anticipated (Fig. 4bi). The average particle length was around 20 nm, and they were uniformly coated with a single layer of dopamine. In contrast, HRTEM images of CDs displayed spherical nanodots with an average diameter of 5–7 nm (Fig. 4aii). The SAED pattern for the CDs showed diffraction rings, indicating that the particles were poorly crystalline (Fig. S3b). The morphology of CDs was further confirmed using FESEM imaging, which was consistent with the HRTEM results. The CDs appeared uniform and spherical, with an average particle size of approximately 6 nm (Fig. 4bii). The minimal aggregation observed further supports the well-distributed nature of the particles in the solution. However, the nanocomposites of CDs and Dop-CuS NPs did not exhibit significant morphological changes.

HRTEM images reveal a linear assembly of CDs on the outer layer of CuS NPs (Fig. 4aiii). The SAED pattern indicates the formation of polycrystalline particles in the nanocomposites (Fig. S3c). Additionally, FESEM analysis confirmed the surface morphology of the functional supramolecular nanocomposites, showing small carbon dots completely covering the outer layer of dopamine-functionalized CuS NPs (Fig. 4biii). The interfacial properties of dopamine facilitate the formation of a sticky layer on the surface of CuS NPs, promoting strong adhesion with the CDs and resulting in stable CDs@Dop-CuS nanocomposites. EDX spectrum of Dop-CuS NPs indicated the presence of Cu (40.19 %) and S (59.81 %) ions in a 2:3 ratio, with minimal contamination from other elements (Fig. S4ai). The higher percentage of S²⁻ ions are likely due to the adsorption of additional S²⁻ ions from the solution onto the nanoparticle surfaces. EDX analysis of the CDs showed atomic percentages of 73.60 % carbon, 19.00 % oxygen, and 6.50 % nitrogen (Fig. S4aii) [49]. For the CDs@Dop-CuS nanocomposite, EDX results indicated the presence of carbon, oxygen, nitrogen, copper, and sulfur with atomic percentages of 29.24 %, 42.01 %, 3.94 %, 10.18 %, and 14.44 %, respectively (Fig. S4aiii). In addition, the elemental composition was confirmed through elemental mapping. The results indicated an even distribution of all elements: Cu and S in Dop-CuS NPs, and C, N, and O in CDs. For the CDs@Dop-CuS nanocomposite, elemental mapping showed a uniform distribution of C, N, S, Cu, and O (Fig. 4ci, cii, ciii). These results further supported the absence of impurities in the synthesized particles.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Antibacterial potential of nanoparticles against E. coli
ce:section.ce:para: The antibacterial properties of the synthesized nanoparticles were validated using the standard colony-forming unit (CFU) counting method, specifically targeting Gram-negative bacteria. Serial dilutions of bacterial suspensions (10−4) were prepared, and different concentrations of the nanoparticles were added. Subsequently, 50 μL of each bacterial suspension were uniformly spread onto agar plates. A bacterial suspension without nanoparticles served as the negative control. Throughout the experiment, the temperature was maintained at 37±1°C and humidity at 50–55 % to ensure consistent environment. All plates were incubated for 24 h under these conditions. Post-incubation, bacterial colonies were counted manually and the results indicated that the antibacterial effect was more pronounced for the CDs@Dop-CuS nanocomposite compared to Dop-CuS NPs and CDs alone. Notably, significant antibacterial activity was observed at higher nanoparticle concentrations, particularly at 200 μg/mL. As depicted in Fig. 5, the number of bacterial colonies following treatment with the CDs@Dop-CuS nanocomposite was markedly lower compared to the control, CDs, and Dop-CuS NPs, demonstrating superior antibacterial efficacy. The antibacterial activity of different nanomaterials was approximated by bacterial inhibition percentage (Eq. 4). In the CDs@Dop-CuS nanocomposite, the bacterial inhibition percentage increased consistently with rising nanoparticle concentrations, ranging from 50 to 200 μg/mL, as shown in Table S1. This trend was also observed for Dop-CuS NPs and CDs; however, the bacterial sensitivity and viability were comparatively lower with these materials. At a concentration of 200 μg/mL, the bacterial inhibition percentages were 94.5 %, 93.15 %, and 97.9 % for Dop-CuS NPs, CDs, and CDs@Dop-CuS nanocomposite, respectively [50]. Qualitative results further demonstrated that the nanocomposite treatment significantly reduced bacterial cell proliferation compared to other treatments.

Based on these findings, it is reasonable to deduce that the synthesized nanocomposite is highly stable and exhibits enhanced antibacterial capacity relative to the other nanomaterials (Fig. 5). Given these superior properties, the nanocomposite was further utilized in the photodynamic therapy of bacterial cells. Previous studies have shown that photosensitizers alone are often insufficient for successful antibacterial therapies due to their self-aggregation behavior under different pH conditions [51]. Therefore, the developed nanocomposite was modelled for the targeted delivery of methylene blue to E. coli bacterial cells, indicating remarkable potential for therapeutic applications. Subsequently, the photosensitizer MB was successfully loaded onto the surface of the therapeutic nanocarrier, CDs@Dop-CuS.

The encapsulation efficacy of MB was approximated by analysing bare MB and MB loaded CDs@Dop-CuS nanocomposite under fluorescence spectrophotometer. For comparison the MB was also loaded onto the surface of Dop-CuS NPs and CDs in order to approximate their loading potential. As depicted in Fig. S5a, the loaded MB displays a sharp decline in the fluorescence peak on treatment with Dop-CuS NPs, CDs and CDs@Dop-CuS nanocomposite. The percent MB encapsulation was estimated and the results depicted 59.5 % loading efficacy of MB over the surface of CDs@Dop-CuS nanocomposite. Moreover, Dop-CuS NPs and CDs exhibited 51.02 % and 41.80 % encapsulation efficiency for MB. Hence the integrated system acts as a versatile platform for therapeutic applications like PDT. Moreover, the stability of the bare MB and MB/CDs@Dop-CuS nanocomposite was investigated under continuous laser light exposure. The results demonstrated that bare MB exhibited aggregative behavior, whereas the MB/CDs@Dop-CuS nanocomposite displayed superior stability, with no observable signs of aggregation (Fig. S5b). These results highlight the superior effects of the MB/CDs@Dop-CuS nanocomposite, particularly in subsequent experiments on bacterial cells, where its enhanced stability under continuous laser exposure is likely to contribute to improved antibacterial performance.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Singlet oxygen production under light irradiation
ce:section.ce:para: To obtain a quantitative analysis of light activated bactericidal action, and generation of singlet oxygen by the photosensitizer, two distinct methods, namely indirect and direct method were used. The indirect method involves the generation of singlet oxygen species by light irradiation upon the photosensitizer (MB) while the direct method involves the operation of laser irradiated photosensitizer on the bacterial cells. In indirect approach, a chemical probe i.e. uric acid has been used to estimate the production of singlet oxygen from photosensitizer and the results were estimated via monitoring the changes in the absorbance spectra of uric acid [52]. The photosensitization mechanism was investigated by obtaining the UV–vis. spectra of uric acid in the presence of MB under different light irradiation ( Fig. 6). The effect of dark conditions was first approximated for all the combinations of MB loaded nanoparticles (Fig. 6a). The outcomes indicated that uric acid exhibited no significant reduction in absorption spectra under dark conditions (Fig. 6a). Additionally, the uric acid absorbance decay was verified by the photoactivation of both uric acid and photosensitizer (bare MB, MB/Dop-CuS NPs, MB/CDs and MB/CDs@Dop-CuS nanocomposite) using light sources corresponding to their absorption wavelength i.e. UV light (200–390 nm) and laser diode (660 nm) respectively (Table S2).

The results showed that uric acid is incapable of degrading under UV light irradiation (Fig. 6b), Hence its decay is directly related to the generation of singlet oxygen species from MB dye. Noticeably, the photosensitizer (MB) illumination with laser light (λ=660 nm) in the presence of uric acid engenders the oxidation of uric acid, thereby resulting in the absorbance decay at 292 nm (Fig. 6c). The obtained results showed that the amount of singlet oxygen production was comparatively more in MB/CDs@Dop-CuS nanocomposite followed by MB/Dop-CuS NPs, MB/CDs, and bare MB. With the perspective of enhanced singlet oxygen generation exhibited by nanocomposite, the system was further tested by direct method in concordance with colony formation units in E. coli bacterial cells. In order to make a clear assessment of antibacterial photodynamic effect, the different nanomaterials i.e. Dop-CuS NPs, CDs and CDs@Dop-CuS nanocomposite were analysed for colony formation units under dark conditions at distinct concentrations ( Fig. 7a). The results justified that the colony formation units were minimum in case of CDs@Dop-CuS nanocomposite followed by Dop-CuS NPs and CDs at each concentration (Fig. 7ci). Using 100 μg/mL as standard amount, an experiment was conducted with the nanocomposite loaded with MB under dark conditions, taking bare MB as control (Fig. 7cii).

These outcomes suggest that the MB loaded CDs@Dop-CuS nanocomposite indicates a sharp decrease in the colony formation units in contrast to the studies performed in the absence of MB. The obtained results conclude that the presence of photosensitizer leads to generation of some radical species which effectively assassinates the bacterial cells. The final measurements on singlet oxygen generation and its consequential effect on bacterial cells were approximated by irradiating the mixture of MB/CDs@Dop-CuS nanocomposite and bacterial cells with laser light diode (Fig. 7b). A control experiment was performed with the light irradiation on solution containing E. coli cells and MB alone (Fig. 7ciii). The outcomes verdicts the excellent performance of MB/CDs@Dop-CuS nanocomposite on bacterial cells as almost zero CFU was observed on the agar plate (Fig. 7b). However, the control solution exhibited a small amount of CFU growth which evidences the less generation of singlet oxygen species in MB alone in contrast to MB loaded CDs@Dop-CuS nanocomposite (Fig. S6a). The relative cell viability of MB-treated bacterial cells was found to be 12.78 % and 6.84 % under dark and light conditions, respectively. In contrast, the cell viability of MB/CDs@Dop-CuS nanocomposite-treated cells was equivalent to 9.58 % and 0.91 % under dark and light conditions, respectively (Table S3). Comparing the cell viability results with uric acid-based results revealed that bare MB exhibits 0 % and 60 % of singlet oxygen production in dark and laser irradiation while the MB/CDs@Dop-CuS nanocomposite showed 0.2 % and 79 % respectively (Table S2). The pronounced difference in singlet oxygen generation between the direct and indirect approaches is attributed to distinct mechanisms. In the direct approach, significant bacterial inhibition likely arises from multiple factors, including DNA binding, inhibition of bacterial enzymes, electrostatic interactions with the bacterial membrane, and the production of reactive oxygen species (ROS). Conversely, in the indirect approach, although MB still undergoes redox reactions with uric acid, the uric acid effectively neutralizes the ROS before they can accumulate. As a result, negligible singlet oxygen production was observed in the presence of uric acid, whereas substantial singlet oxygen production occurred in bacterial cells, leading to a marked antibacterial effect. Further, the synthesised nanocomposite was assessed for its recyclability by obtaining the sample after each uric acid-based experiment. The outcomes show that the nano photosensitiser retains more than 80 % efficacy even after five consecutive cycles (Fig. S6b). Table S4 represents a detailed comparison of the current biochemically synthesized nanocomposite with existing photodynamic therapy agents.

The mechanism associated with singlet oxygen production has been illustrated in Fig. 8a. When the photosensitizer was illuminated with laser light, its ground state electrons are excited into first singlet excited state. The excited singlet state undergoes intersystem crossing (ISC), forming a metastable triplet electronic excited state where it transfers its excess energy to the oxygen moieties (3O2). This interaction generates singlet oxygen species (1O2) which reacts with uric acid to produce sodium oxanate, triuret, CO2 and allantoxaidin [52]. Additionally, the photosensitizer in the triplet oxygen state may lead to formation of radical ions, further contributing to the production of oxidized products, accountable for the degradation of uric acid. As evidenced by the results, the characteristic singlet oxygen generation was higher in case of MB loaded CDs@Dop-CuS nanocomposite in contrast to bare MB (Fig. 7b). This enhanced production can be attributed to the fact that when excited under laser light, CuS nanoparticles effectively transfers energy to carbon dots, due to small size, unique surface chemistry, and enhanced reactivity between them. This process results in boosted production of ROS such as singlet oxygen, hydroxyl radical, H2O2 etc, thereby promoting more antibacterial effect. Further, the Cu2+ ions in the nanocomposite acts as electron donor or acceptor which prolongs the lifetime of triplet excited state, leading to more ROS production. Consequently, the MB/CDs@Dop-CuS photosensitizer spends longer time in triplet excited state in contrast to bare MB which enhances the photodynamic effect in bacterial cells (Fig. 8a). However, this effect was quite negligible in case of bare MB treated bacterial cells, leading to the production of lesser singlet oxygen species. Additionally, the augmented PDT effect is also promoted by the efficacious control on the aggregation behavior of MB during the outward release from the nanocomposite [53]. Based on these findings, singlet oxygen production was quantified for all the samples, showing that the enhanced ROS generation could be ascribed to the prolonged triplet excited state of the nanophotosensitizer (Fig. 7b). The FESEM analysis is an important aspect for studying the morphological behavior of bacterial cells treated with bare MB and MB/CDs@Dop-CuS nanocomposite. Fig. 8b displays the FESEM images of E. coli bacterial cells before and after treatment with laser irradiated MB and MB loaded CDs@Dop-CuS nanocomposite. The E. coli images obtained from the control solution (cells without treatment) displayed well established rod like morphology with a smooth and regular appearance around the cell wall (Fig. 8bi). This indicates highly viable state of bacteria which on treatment exhibits collapsing and disintegration of cell wall. From the FESEM images, it is evident that the MB/CDs@Dop-CuS nanocomposite caused complete bacterial death by adhering to the outermost layer of the bacterial cell, thereby disrupting the entire bacterial cell wall through the generation of radical species upon light irradiation (Fig. 8biii). While in case of bare MB treated bacterial cells, lesser distortion was observed due to reduced activity of aggregated MB molecules, which fails to produce enough 1O2 and get transformed into its isomeric form (Leuko isomer) (Fig. 8bii) [54].

The mechanism involved in the disruption of cell morphology in nanocomposite treated bacterial cells could further be correlated with high electrostatic interaction between the negatively charged E. coli bacterial cells and positively charged MB dye molecules loaded over the surface of CDs@Dop-CuS nanocomposite. Upon integration of MB molecules over the bacterial cell wall, the MB molecules generate enough singlet oxygen species upon laser light irradiation. These singlet oxygen species are responsible for killing the bacterial cells, hence known as photodynamic effect (Fig. 8a). Table S3 depicts the reduced viability of bacterial cells on treatment with MB/CDs@Dop-CuS nanocomposite.

In recent decades, molecular docking study was utilized to understand the biochemical pathways and the enzymes responsible for it. The molecular docking studies analysis the binding capacity and inhibition potential of Dop-CuS NPs, CDs and their composite i.e. CDs@Dop-CuS as well as indicates the underlying biological interaction between the enzymes and nanocomposite (
Figs. 9 and 10). The enzymes β-lactamase and DNA gyrase are the two potential enzymes found in E. coli bacterial cells and were selected as plausible target for the antibacterial mechanism.

The optimally docked conformation of β-lactamase exhibited conventional hydrogen bonding interaction with Leu117, Ala114, Gly115, Gly116 in Dop-CuS NPs, Lys84, Pro306, Trp276 in CDs and Asp123, Gly321, Gln120 in CDs@CuS-Dop nanocomposite (Fig. 9). The binding score attained for the best docked conformation in case of Dop-CuS NPs, CDs, and CDs@CuS-Dop nanocomposite was found to be −1.48, −9.71 and −11.04 kcal/mol. For DNA gyrase, the CDs@CuS-Dop nanocomposite demonstrated a robust binding potential of −13.28 kcal/mol, primarily attributed to hydrogen bonding interactions with key residues such as Gln135, Thr62, Arg168, and Glu131 (Fig. 10).

Comparable to CDs@CuS-Dop nanocomposite, Dop-CuS NPs and CDs shows the binding score of −1.45 and −9.11 kcal/mol attributed to the hydrogen bonding interaction with Gly33, Asp29, Leu36 and Arg76, Arg136, Ala100 of DNA gyrase respectively. Fig. 9 represents the association between protein molecules and ligands, and their associated ligand receptor hydrogen bond interactions. Therefore, CDs@CuS-Dop nanocomposite has been validated as excellent photosensitizer for the antibacterial activity against E. coli bacterial cells.

In summary, we have developed simple and facile method for the preparation of CDs from Aegle marmelos fruit exocarp. This system offers significant advantages when combined electrostatically with Dop-CuS nanoparticles, leading to the formation of a robust CDs@Dop-CuS nanocomposite. The resulting nanocomposite exhibits excellent photostability and serves as a crucial intermediate for the delivery and activation of the photosensitizer. Notably, it facilitates the direct conversion of less stable photosensitizer into a more potent, durable, and stable form within the MB-loaded CDs@Dop-CuS nanocomposite, demonstrating an encapsulation efficiency of 59.5 %. Furthermore, the resultant complex displays a more efficient photodynamic therapy effect with enhanced singlet oxygen production i.e. 79 % compared to MB alone i.e. 60 %. Subsequently, the impact on E. coli bacterial cells was assessed through FESEM imaging, revealing maximal distortion of bacterial cells upon treatment with the MB-loaded nanocomposite, indicative of heightened singlet oxygen species production. To elucidate the binding interaction between the nanocomposite and bacteria, molecular docking studies were conducted, revealing the nanocomposite's lowest binding energy of −11.04 and −13.28 kcal/mol with β-lactamase and DNA gyrase. This underscores the potential of our approach in facilitating the efficient in vivo action of the photosensitizer. The results underscore the remarkable photodynamic effect exhibited by the MB-loaded CDs@Dop-CuS nanocomposite, offering a promising avenue for further exploration in biomedical applications. Future research will focus on evaluating the developed photosensitizer across a broader spectrum of bacterial strains and cancer cell lines to ensure its efficacy and safety for potential real-world applications in public health, water purification, and environmental remediation.
